title: Four anchors: how I hold a day together without willpower
content:
There is a version of my morning routine that fills three pages. I've written it. It has habit stacks and review rituals and optimised blocks. It also collapses completely the moment I wake up wrong — which, with a cycling mood, is not a rare event.

So I scrapped it. What I kept was four things. Not four pages. Four anchors.

An anchor is different from a habit. A habit is something you do when you're doing well. An anchor is something that holds when you aren't. The test of an anchor is simple: can I execute this on my worst day, half-awake, with no motivation and a flat mood? If yes, it's an anchor. If no, it's a nice idea.

**Anchor one: fixed wake time.** Same time, seven days a week, alarm out of reach. This one is non-negotiable because it is the strongest single regulator of a cycling mood — more than diet, more than exercise, more than any supplement. The research on social rhythm therapy is clear: the body clock stabilises the mood clock, and the body clock runs off a consistent wake signal. Sleeping in on Sunday isn't rest. It's borrowing against the week.

**Anchor two: meds, water, light.** On waking, before any decision. The Concerta goes in, a full glass of water follows, and I get daylight on my eyes — five minutes by a window or outside. This takes four minutes and anchors the day's chemistry before my brain has any say in the matter. You can't negotiate with a disordered dopamine system. You can preempt it.

**Anchor three: one real meal.** Not the best meal, not a tracked meal — a real one, at a roughly fixed time. A meal time is a rhythm cue before it is nutrition. Skipping it doesn't just mean I'm hungry. It means I removed a clock signal my nervous system was counting on. The hunger I notice. The destabilisation I don't, until later.

**Anchor four: one line written.** "Today I am ___. Today the one thing is ___." Two blanks. Thirty seconds. This names my actual state — not the state I want to have, my real one — so I can route the day accordingly. It also commits to one thing, not ten. One thing I will do, even on a crash day. Everything beyond that is bonus.

Four anchors. On a good day I build something on top of them. On a bad day they *are* the day — and the day still counts. The streak doesn't break. The body clock holds. The chemistry gets its head start.

The whole system you read about, the 500-word essays, the automation, the outreach — all of it stands on this floor. Get the floor right, and the rest can be built when you're ready. Neglect it, and nothing else holds for long.

Anchors first. Always.

title: How I teach IELTS writing with an ADHD brain (and why it makes me better at it)
content:
My ADHD makes me a better IELTS writing teacher. I didn't believe it for years. Now I'd put it on a business card.

Here's why. IELTS Task 2 — the academic essay — has a specific failure mode that kills most students' scores. They know the grammar. They have the vocabulary. They write five hundred words and score 5.5 anyway. The examiner's comment: "not sufficiently focused," "ideas not clearly developed," "coherence breaks down." The student reads it and blames their English. Their English is fine. The problem is structure.

Structure is invisible to most people. To me it is the only thing I see.

An ADHD brain cannot hold a loose argument together. Too many threads, too much interesting detail, too easy to follow a tangent off a cliff. So very early on I built a scaffold I could use *without relying on working memory* — a skeleton so rigid that even my most scattered day couldn't collapse it. Position statement → two supporting points → each point: claim, example, explanation, link back. Full stop. No detours.

That scaffold is now what I teach. Not because it's the only way to write — it isn't. Because it is the fastest way to a Band 7 for a student who has twelve weeks, a job, and no patience for theory. It is constraint as liberation.

The lesson itself runs in twenty-five minutes. I give the student a question. They write one sentence of position. We check it: does it answer *all parts* of the question? No hedging, no "there are many opinions" — a direct answer in one sentence. Then they write the two supporting sentences, one per body paragraph, before they write the paragraphs themselves. A plan that takes three minutes and prevents ninety percent of coherence problems.

Then they write. I don't correct grammar in real time — that switches their brain to editing mode and kills output. I read the draft for one thing only: does each sentence serve the point it's supposed to serve? If it doesn't, the sentence goes, regardless of how well-written it is. This is the hardest thing to learn. Students fall in love with their sentences. Good examiners don't care about sentences; they care about argument.

After Cambridge, after CELTA, after hundreds of hours in front of students, this is what I know: the brain that cannot rely on working memory learns to make the structure external, visible, and uncheat-able. That's a gift, not a deficit — and it turns out most students need exactly that.

Band 7 is not a mystery. It's a repeatable skeleton, executed cleanly. I've seen it work every time.

title: Teaching English from the edge of the map (and why it's an advantage)
content:
La Réunion sits in the Indian Ocean, 9,000 kilometres from Paris and about as far from the London publishing world as a French territory can get. When I tell people I'm an English trainer here, the first reaction is usually sympathy — as if distance were a handicap. It isn't. It's a specific advantage I've spent years learning to use.

The island has a particular need: French professionals who must operate in English — for business, for qualifications, for careers that extend beyond the island — but who have almost no access to native-level English instruction grounded in a French-speaking context. The mainland has London within two hours. La Réunion has me.

That's a small market, you might think. But small and underserved beats large and saturated every time. The IELTS student in Paris competes for attention with a thousand tutors. The professional in Saint-Denis who needs a TOEIC for their promotion has considerably fewer options. I am often the specific option.

And then there's the remote layer. My CELTA, my IELTS preparation expertise, my Business English background — none of it requires a physical classroom. A Zoom link and a strong connection (and La Réunion's fibre is surprisingly good) opens the entirety of the French-speaking world. Antilles, French Guiana, Senegal, Morocco, Côte d'Ivoire — all markets where English is an economic accelerator and quality instruction in a bilingual context is rare.

The interesting thing about being far from the centre is that it forces you to be very specific about what you offer. You can't rely on footfall or proximity. You have to be the person who does a particular thing well enough that people seek you out despite the distance. That clarity — knowing exactly what you do and for whom — turns out to be exactly what makes an online practice work.

I'm a CELTA-certified English trainer who speaks French, who has lived the French administrative system from the inside, and who teaches from a place most of my students will never visit but which gives me, daily, the experience of being a professional operating across languages and cultures. That's not a limitation. It's the pitch.

The edge of the map is not the end of the map. For some things — remote teaching, written work, specialised expertise — it's just a slightly unusual home for a practice that exists online anyway.

I stopped explaining the distance. I started using it.

title: Understanding Trauma Treatment: Why Your Brain Needs to Learn Healing
content:
I used to think trauma was something you could think your way out of. That if you just understand what happened, you'll be fine. That willpower and positive thinking fix everything.

I was wrong.

Last month, my psychiatrist handed me a treatment plan. Not a prescription for a pill that fixes everything, but a framework: psychiatric follow-up, psychoeducation on trauma, then specialist trauma therapy. Reading it felt like finally getting instructions for a puzzle I've been trying to solve by throwing pieces at the wall.

## Your Brain Learned to Protect You

Here's what I learned: trauma isn't a weakness. It's a protection mechanism your nervous system learned when you needed it most.

When I experienced childhood abuse and abandonment, my brain got really good at one thing: **staying alert**. Scanning for danger. Never trusting. Freezing when threatened. These were survival strategies. They kept me alive.

The problem? My nervous system never got the message that it's safe now.

Thirty years later, my brain still treats routine interactions like threats. A raised voice → flashback to violence. A breakup → abandonment panic. My hands tremble. I dissociate. I can't sleep. My body is still fighting battles from decades ago.

Willpower doesn't fix this. Understanding it helps, but it doesn't reprogram your nervous system.

## Healing Requires Relearning

This is where actual trauma therapy comes in. Not talk therapy where you just repeat your story until you're numb to it. But evidence-based treatments designed to help your *brain* reprocess trauma.

**EMDR** (Eye Movement Desensitisation & Reprocessing) sounds strange: you recall painful memories while moving your eyes side-to-side. But neuroscience shows this works. It helps your brain process trauma like any other memory instead of keeping it "stuck" as a present threat.

**Trauma-focused CBT** teaches your nervous system that the threat is in the *past*, not the *present*. Gradually, safely, with a trained therapist.

**Somatic therapy** works directly with your body—the tremors, the panic, the dissociation. Because trauma lives in your nervous system, not just your mind.

## The Hard Work of Getting Better

What nobody tells you: healing from trauma is not inspirational. It's not a Netflix montage. It's showing up every week to a therapist and deliberately revisiting the worst things that happened to you. It's trembling in their office. It's sometimes getting worse before you get better.

But here's what I'm learning: it works. Not because of willpower. Not because of positivity. But because trauma therapy literally rewires how your nervous system responds to threat.

## You're Not Broken

I wrote this because if you're like me—if you're struggling with trauma, if nothing seems to work, if you keep retraumatizing yourself trying to think your way to healing—you need to know: this is normal. Your nervous system learned these patterns for a reason.

And the good news? It can learn something new.

The path looks like:
1. **Understanding** what trauma does to your brain (psychoeducation)
2. **Working with a specialist** to reprocess those memories (EMDR, CBT, somatic therapy)
3. **Rebuilding trust** in your body and the world

It's not fast. It's not easy. But it's real healing, not just coping.

Your brain is not broken. It's just been protecting you the only way it knew how. Now it's time to teach it that safety is possible.

---

*If you're considering trauma therapy, start by talking to your doctor. Specialized trauma units exist specifically for this. You don't have to do this alone.*
